data.title: Is Freshworks (FRSH) Starting To Look Interesting After A 36.8% Share Price Slide?
data.description:

  • The stock has seen a 4.6% decline over the last 7 days and is down 31.6% year to date, with a 36.8% decline over the past year that may have changed how the market views its risk and reward profile.
  • Recent headlines around Freshworks have focused on its position within the broader software sector and shifting sentiment toward growth oriented names. This helps explain some of the pressure on the share price. Investors are watching how the company responds to this backdrop, including any updates on its product focus, customer trends, or cost discipline.
  • Freshworks currently scores 4 out of 6 on a set of valuation checks. The next sections will walk through what different valuation methods say about the stock, before finishing with a way to look at value that goes beyond the usual ratios and models.

Approach 1: Freshworks Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today to arrive at an implied value per share. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in today’s money.

For Freshworks, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is around $225.1 million, with analyst and extrapolated estimates pointing to free cash flow of $326.9 million in 2028 and further projections out to 2035. Simply Wall St uses analyst inputs where available, then extends the forecast path to build a 10 year cash flow stream.

When those cash flows are discounted back, the resulting intrinsic value from this DCF comes out at about $24.37 per share. Against the recent share price of $7.94, this implies a 67.4% discount, which points to Freshworks trading materially below this estimate of fair value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Freshworks Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to link what you pay today with the earnings the business is already generating. It gives you a simple snapshot of how many dollars investors are paying for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

Freshworks currently trades on a P/E of 12.28x. That sits below both the Software industry average of 27.81x and the peer group average of 43.51x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Freshworks is 4.66x, which is its proprietary view of what a reasonable P/E might be after accounting for factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk profile.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a plain industry or peer comparison because it adjusts for the specific characteristics of Freshworks rather than assuming all software names should trade on similar multiples. The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently a bank turns its equity base into earnings after covering the required return that shareholders expect. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it centers on return on equity and what that implies for long term value.

The Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value using a required return.

For GFL Environmental, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about CA$209.7m.
